contraintes: requested in-place, byte-level modification of objects on an object storage component
conflicts.explanation: Object storage systems inherently do not support in-place or byte-level modification of objects. They are designed for write-once, read-many (WORM) operations, where objects are replaced entirely rather than modified partially.
A single criterion blocks: relaxing it is enough.

relaxations.cible: The request for in-place, byte-level modification of objects in an object storage system conflicts with the inherent limitations of object storage, which does not support such operations.
relaxations.action: Replace object storage with block storage: Replace the `bucket_data` object storage component with a block storage volume (e.g., AWS EBS, Azure Managed Disk, or GCP Persistent Disk).; Configure the block storage volume with a size of 1 TB and a maximum size of 2 TB to match the original requirements.; Attach the block storage volume to a compute instance to enable in-place, byte-level modifications.
relaxations.explanation: Gains: Enables in-place, byte-level modifications of data, fulfilling the user's requirement. Sacrifices: Loses the scalability, durability, and cost-effectiveness of object storage for large-scale data. Block storage may also introduce additional management overhead (e.g., attaching to compute instances).

relaxations.cible: The request for in-place, byte-level modification of objects in an object storage system conflicts with the inherent limitations of object storage, which does not support such operations.
relaxations.action: Use a database or file system overlay: Deploy a database (e.g., PostgreSQL, MySQL) or a distributed file system (e.g., CephFS, GlusterFS) on top of compute instances.; Configure the database or file system to store data in a way that supports in-place, byte-level modifications.; Use the `bucket_data` object storage only for backup or archival purposes, while the database or file system handles active modifications.
relaxations.explanation: Gains: Enables in-place, byte-level modifications while retaining object storage for backup or archival use. Sacrifices: Introduces additional complexity and cost for managing the database or file system. Performance may also be impacted depending on the solution chosen.

relaxations.cible: The request for in-place, byte-level modification of objects in an object storage system conflicts with the inherent limitations of object storage, which does not support such operations.
relaxations.action: Simulate modifications using object versioning: Enable object versioning on the `bucket_data` object storage component.; Implement a process to download the object, modify it locally, and upload the new version to replace the old one.; Use metadata or a separate tracking system to manage byte-level changes and version history.
relaxations.explanation: Gains: Retains the benefits of object storage (scalability, durability, and cost-effectiveness) while simulating modifications. Sacrifices: Does not provide true in-place, byte-level modifications, which may introduce latency and complexity for applications requiring frequent updates.

relaxations.cible: volume_data (block_storage) — The requested block storage volume size of 1 TB (1024 GB) is not available from any provider, as the maximum size offered is 1000 GB.
relaxations.action: Reduce the storage size requirement: Lower the minimum capacity of the block storage volume from 1024 GB to 1000 GB, which is the maximum size available from providers.
relaxations.explanation: You gain access to real provider offers for block storage, but the total usable capacity is reduced by 24 GB (from 1 TB to 1000 GB). If this reduction is acceptable, this is the simplest solution.

relaxations.cible: volume_data (block_storage) — The requested block storage volume size of 1 TB (1024 GB) is not available from any provider, as the maximum size offered is 1000 GB.
relaxations.action: Split storage into multiple volumes: Replace the single 1 TB volume with two 500 GB block storage volumes (or another combination that sums to at least 1 TB), as 500 GB volumes are widely available.
relaxations.explanation: You retain the total capacity of 1 TB or more, but you must manage multiple volumes at the application level, which adds complexity to your storage architecture.

relaxations.cible: volume_data (block_storage) — The requested block storage volume size of 1 TB (1024 GB) is not available from any provider, as the maximum size offered is 1000 GB.
relaxations.action: Use a provider-specific high-capacity offering: Manually verify if any provider offers block storage volumes larger than 1000 GB in specific regions or under special pricing tiers. If found, adjust the region or budget constraints to accommodate this.
relaxations.explanation: You may achieve the full 1 TB capacity, but this could limit your choice of providers or regions and may increase costs. This option requires additional research and potential adjustments to other constraints.

contraintes: Co-localisation ET anti-colocalisation simultanées des deux mêmes composants.
conflicts.explanation: Il est logiquement impossible pour deux composants d'être à la fois co-localisés (même emplacement) et anti-colocalisés (emplacements différents). Ces deux contraintes sont mutuellement exclusives et ne peuvent être satisfaites simultanément.

relaxations.cible: Il est impossible pour deux composants d'être simultanément co-localisés (même emplacement) et anti-colocalisés (emplacements différents).
relaxations.action: Abandonner la contrainte de co-localisation: Supprimer la contrainte exigeant que les deux composants soient co-localisés (même emplacement).
relaxations.explanation: Les deux composants pourront être déployés dans des emplacements différents, ce qui résout le conflit. En revanche, la perte de co-localisation peut entraîner une latence accrue ou des coûts de communication plus élevés entre les composants.

relaxations.cible: Il est impossible pour deux composants d'être simultanément co-localisés (même emplacement) et anti-colocalisés (emplacements différents).
relaxations.action: Abandonner la contrainte d'anti-colocalisation: Supprimer la contrainte exigeant que les deux composants soient anti-colocalisés (emplacements différents).
relaxations.explanation: Les deux composants pourront être déployés dans le même emplacement, ce qui résout le conflit. Cependant, cela peut réduire la résilience ou la tolérance aux pannes, car une défaillance locale pourrait impacter les deux composants simultanément.

relaxations.cible: Il est impossible pour deux composants d'être simultanément co-localisés (même emplacement) et anti-colocalisés (emplacements différents).
relaxations.action: Redéfinir les contraintes de localisation: Remplacer les contraintes de co-localisation et d'anti-colocalisation par une contrainte de proximité contrôlée, par exemple : 'Les deux composants doivent être déployés dans la même région mais dans des zones de disponibilité différentes'.
relaxations.explanation: Cette approche permet de concilier partiellement les exigences de performance (proximité) et de résilience (séparation physique limitée). Cependant, cela ne satisfait pleinement ni la co-localisation ni l'anti-colocalisation initiales.

conflicts.explanation: A multi-zone deployment with a 99.99% availability SLA requires redundant infrastructure across at least two availability zones, including failover mechanisms and monitoring. Such a setup universally costs significantly more than 50 €/month, making these constraints mutually unsatisfiable.
A single criterion blocks: relaxing it is enough.

relaxations.cible: The requested 99.99% multi-zone SLA for PostgreSQL is incompatible with the 50 €/month cost cap.
relaxations.action: Increase the cost budget to accommodate multi-zone redundancy: Raise the monthly cost cap from 50 € to at least 200 € to support redundant virtual machines, block storage, and failover mechanisms across two availability zones.
relaxations.explanation: The architecture meets the 99.99% multi-zone SLA, but the monthly cost increases beyond the original 50 € limit.

relaxations.cible: The requested 99.99% multi-zone SLA for PostgreSQL is incompatible with the 50 €/month cost cap.
relaxations.action: Reduce availability requirements to single-zone: Replace the 99.99% multi-zone SLA with a 99.9% single-zone SLA, eliminating the need for redundant infrastructure.; Retain the 50 €/month cost cap.
relaxations.explanation: The architecture remains within the 50 €/month budget, but the availability SLA drops to 99.9% (single-zone), increasing downtime risk.

relaxations.cible: The requested 99.99% multi-zone SLA for PostgreSQL is incompatible with the 50 €/month cost cap.
relaxations.action: Use a managed database service with shared redundancy: Replace the self-managed PostgreSQL on vm_postgres with a cloud-provider-managed PostgreSQL service (e.g., Azure Database for PostgreSQL) configured for zone-redundant availability.; Retain the 99.99% multi-zone SLA and adjust the cost cap to 150 €/month.
relaxations.explanation: The architecture meets the 99.99% multi-zone SLA with reduced operational overhead, but the cost cap must be raised to 150 €/month.

relaxations.cible: vm_postgres_primary (compute) — No cloud provider in the eastus region offers a virtual machine with a 99.99% availability SLA for a single availability zone.
relaxations.action: Lower the availability requirement: Reduce the SLA requirement from 99.99% to 99.9% (the highest available for single-zone deployments in eastus).
relaxations.explanation: You gain access to real offers for your primary virtual machine, but the uptime guarantee drops from 99.99% (52.56 minutes of downtime per year) to 99.9% (8.76 hours of downtime per year). Multi-zone redundancy remains unaffected.

relaxations.cible: vm_postgres_secondary (compute) — No cloud provider in the eastus region offers a virtual machine with a 99.99% availability SLA for a single availability zone.
relaxations.action: Lower the availability requirement: Reduce the SLA requirement from 99.99% to 99.9% (the highest available for single-zone deployments in eastus).
relaxations.explanation: You gain access to real offers for your secondary virtual machine, but the uptime guarantee drops from 99.99% (52.56 minutes of downtime per year) to 99.9% (8.76 hours of downtime per year). Multi-zone redundancy remains unaffected.

relaxations.explanation: You gain access to real load balancer offers, but the traffic distribution algorithm changes. 'Layer4' operates at the transport level (faster, simpler), while 'layer7' operates at the application level (more features, slightly higher latency).
